notes.note: Curius spins a legal-philosophical conceit out of the Greek terms chresis (use) and ktesis (ownership): Cicero holds the usufruct of him while Atticus holds title, as with a slave whose use is leased out. The whole sign-off plays on the language of Roman property law.

Latin senes comptionales: worthless old slaves sold off in a job lot (coemptio) for a token price, a stock joke for human goods nobody wants. Curius means he himself would fetch almost nothing at auction.

Ser. Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51 BC), the great jurist, was governing the province of Achaia (45 BC); Curius, a Roman banker resident at Patrae, asks Cicero to commend him to whoever replaces Sulpicius in the proconsulship.

notes.anchor: whitewashing two walls from the same pot
notes.note: A proverb (duo parietes de eadem fidelia dealbare) for double-dealing: getting two jobs done with one batch of lime, i.e. trying to please two patrons at once. Curius jokes that Atticus must not learn he flatters both his patrons alike.
The Kalends were the first day of the Roman month; the fourth day before them (counting inclusively) is 29 October. This dateline fixes the letter to late October 45 BC.
